subcategory: Western US native dry spring bulb

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall 3–4 inches deep in full sun to partial shade in very well-drained, dry, sandy or gritty soil in zone 3–8; Yellow Fritillary (Yellow Bells, Pudding Onion) is a charming, small, early-spring-blooming native bulb of the western United States — found from British Columbia to California east to the Rockies in sagebrush steppe, bunchgrass prairies, and open pine forest — bearing one to three nodding, bright golden-yellow bell flowers on each short stem, among the first native wildflowers to bloom in late winter and early spring.

directions: Plant Fritillaria pudica bulbs in fall 3–4 inches deep in very well-drained, dry, sandy or gritty soil in full sun; they require dry summer dormancy and are intolerant of summer moisture — grow in raised beds, scree, or containers to control drainage; provide minimal water during winter and spring, then dry out completely by early summer. Yellow Fritillary is a small but disproportionately delightful native bulb — its bright golden-yellow, nodding bells appearing remarkably early in late winter and early spring when little else is in bloom in its native Western sagebrush and bunchgrass habitat. The bulbs are surrounded by numerous rice-grain bulblets that produce offsets and spread into small colonies over several years. Indigenous peoples throughout its range used the small bulbs as a food source — eaten raw, roasted in the coals, or boiled and mixed with other foods. The main challenge in cultivation outside the West is providing the perfectly dry summer dormancy the bulbs require; in humid summer climates, growing in pots that can be stored dry under a bench from July through October is the most reliable approach. Plant among early spring companions like Phlox subulata, spring-blooming Allium, or in a naturalistic Western xeriscape.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Fritillaria pudica

planting.note: Plant corms in fall 3–4 inches deep in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, dry to dry-summer clay or sandy loam in zone 7–10; Harvest Brodiaea is a late-spring to early-summer blooming California native corm — native to grasslands, chaparral margins, and oak woodland openings throughout the Sierra Nevada foothills and coastal ranges — bearing rich, deep blue-violet, funnel-shaped flowers in loose, airy umbels held on long, wiry stems above strap-like leaves that die back well before flowering.

directions: Plant Brodiaea elegans corms in fall 3 inches deep in well-drained, lean soil in full sun; leaves appear in winter, wither before flowering; provide no summer water after foliage dies back — summer drought is essential for corm persistence; naturalize in grassland plantings or combine with other California natives. Harvest Brodiaea blooms after its slender, strap-like leaves have already withered and disappeared — the wiry flower stems and brilliant blue-violet flowers appearing in late spring and early summer above bare soil, creating a cheerful, surprising display in naturalistic plantings where their origins might be forgotten. The rich blue-violet flowers (sometimes described as indigo or deep lavender), held in loose, open umbels of 3–12 individual funnel-shaped blooms, are among the most vivid blues in the native California flora. Corms were historically eaten by California Indigenous peoples, boiled or roasted; they have a mild, starchy flavor. An ideal companion for California poppies, blue grama grass, and other dry-summer natives in water-wise California gardens; once established in the right conditions, they naturalize reliably and require no supplemental water or care. In humid summer climates, grown in containers that can be dried out in summer.
sources: California Native Plant Society — Brodiaea elegans
Theodore Payne Foundation — California native bulbs and corms

planting.note: Plant corms in fall 3 inches deep in full sun in well-drained, lean, dry to summer-dry soil in zone 7–10; Blue Dicks is among the most abundant and widespread of all California wildflowers — carpeting grasslands, chaparral hillsides, and oak savannas from Oregon to Baja California and east to Utah and Arizona — with dense, rounded clusters of small, bright blue-purple to lavender flowers held on long, wiry, leafless stems from late winter through spring, one of the most reliable wildflowers in the California native palette.

directions: Plant Dichelostemma capitatum corms in fall 3 inches deep in any well-drained, lean soil in full sun; they thrive in rocky, clay, or sandy soils as long as drainage is good; provide minimal water in winter and spring (natural rainfall is usually sufficient), then dry completely in summer; corms multiply readily into large colonies. Blue Dicks is one of the most quintessentially Californian of all spring wildflowers — a familiar sight on roadsides, hillsides, and open grasslands throughout the state where it blooms in abundance from February through May. The small, bright blue-purple to lavender, tubular flowers are packed into tight, round clusters (capitulum) at the top of tall, slender, wiry stems that sway gracefully in the breeze — creating a beautiful, airy effect when naturalized in masses. The corms were a major starchy food source for many California Indigenous peoples — eaten raw, roasted in the coals, or boiled; they have a slightly sweet, nutty flavor and were an important caloric staple. Blue Dicks is one of the easier California native corms to grow outside California and adapts to a wider range of summer conditions than many species; in humid summer climates, plant in raised beds or well-drained slopes to ensure good summer drainage. An essential component of California native wildflower meadows and oak savanna restoration plantings.
sources: Native American Ethnobotany Database — Dichelostemma capitatum

planting.note: Plant corms in fall 3–4 inches deep in full sun in well-drained, lean, dry to summer-dry soil in zone 6–10; Ithuriel's Spear is a striking California native corm — native to grasslands, chaparral, and woodland edges in the Central Valley foothills and Coast Ranges — bearing large, loose, open umbels of trumpet-shaped, vivid blue-violet to lavender flowers on tall, wiry stems in late spring and early summer, long after the narrow, strap-like leaves have died back completely.

directions: Plant Triteleia laxa corms in fall 3–4 inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun; leaves emerge in winter and die back by late spring before the flowers appear — mark planting locations carefully; provide no summer water as summer drought is essential; corms naturalize readily and multiply by offsets over several years. Ithuriel's Spear takes its poetic name from John Milton's Paradise Lost, where the angel Ithuriel carries a spear that reveals true forms — a name given to this plant, perhaps, for its dramatic, spear-like, erect flower stems. The loosely arranged umbels of 6–18 trumpet-shaped flowers in vivid blue-violet to pale lavender (occasionally white) are among the most beautiful in the California native flora, appearing in late spring to early summer on tall, wiry, leafless stems at a time when many other spring wildflowers have finished blooming. The cultivar 'Queen Fabiola' is widely available in the trade and has particularly large, rich-violet flowers and reliable performance in cultivation. Unlike many California native bulbs, Triteleia laxa has proven adaptable to some garden irrigation in summer and has naturalized in gardens outside California — though best growth and persistence comes with dry summers. Excellent for cutting; flowers last well in the vase.

subcategory: California native dry summer ornamental onion

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall 2–3 inches deep in full sun in well-drained, dry, lean, rocky to sandy-loam soil in zone 5–9; Narrowleaf Onion (Papery Onion) is a California and Pacific Northwest native — growing in rocky grasslands, hillsides, and serpentine outcrops from British Columbia south through California and east to Nevada — producing small, rounded, papery-white to pale pink flower heads in late spring and early summer above very slender, grass-like leaves.

directions: Plant Allium amplectens bulbs in fall in rocky, well-drained, lean soil in full sun; naturalize in rocky slopes, gravel gardens, or dry native plant meadows; summer drought essential — provide no water from June through September; clumps multiply slowly by offsets. Narrowleaf Onion is a delicate and underused California and Pacific Northwest native ornamental, forming very slender, almost grass-like clumps of leaves surmounted by small, round clusters of papery white to pale blush-pink flowers in late spring. The ultra-narrow foliage and small, neat flower heads create an airy, refined texture that blends gracefully into native grass and wildflower plantings. Like all wild Allium, it has a distinct onion odor when bruised; the bulbs and leaves are edible as a wild onion, used historically by many Pacific Coast Indigenous peoples as a food and seasoning. The dried seed heads are attractive and persist through summer and fall, adding subtle texture to the dry garden. Best grown in thin, rocky soil or gravel — enriched garden soil tends to produce too-lush foliage that flops and reduces flower production. An excellent naturalizing companion for Blue Dicks, Brodiaea, Triteleia, and native bunch grasses.

subcategory: Utah state flower edible Great Basin native bulb

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall 3–4 inches deep in full sun in very well-drained, dry, lean, sandy or gravelly soil in zone 4–9; Sego Lily is the state flower of Utah — a beautiful, tulip-like native bulb of the Great Basin and Rocky Mountain West, found in sagebrush steppe, pinyon-juniper woodland, and dry mountain meadows from North Dakota to California — bearing pristine white to pale lavender flowers with distinctive purple, yellow, and brown markings and historically a critical food source that sustained Mormon settlers through the starvation winter of 1848.

directions: Plant Calochortus nuttallii bulbs in fall 3–4 inches deep in extremely well-drained, lean, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; avoid clay soils or areas with summer moisture; provide spring moisture (natural snowmelt or light irrigation) and dry out completely by early summer; mark locations as summer dormancy makes sites look bare. Sego Lily holds a special place in Western American history — the state flower of Utah, it was designated in 1911 to commemorate its role in saving the lives of Mormon settlers during the winter of 1848 when crops failed and settlers dug and ate the small, starchy bulbs to survive. Indigenous peoples of the Great Basin and Rocky Mountain region had long gathered Sego Lily bulbs as a significant food source — boiled, roasted, or dried for storage, the bulbs have a mild, pleasant starchy flavor. The flowers are among the most beautiful of all Western natives: large, three-petaled, cup-shaped flowers in white to pale lavender or pale pink with a bold purple arch and yellow center markings on each petal, creating an almost jewel-like appearance. Like all Mariposa lilies, the primary cultivation challenge is providing adequate summer drought — growing in containers stored dry in summer or in very freely draining raised beds with gravel mulch. The plants self-sow in ideal conditions and can naturalize in Western dry gardens and prairie-style plantings.
sources: Utah Division of Wildlife Resources — Calochortus nuttallii state flower history
Native American Ethnobotany Database — Calochortus nuttallii food uses
